paragraphs.text: There are many reasons for settling in
: climate, hunting, family, business, and culture. But how about fleeing the long arm of the law? During the 1840s

told his children that at age fourteen he became an apprentice in a furniture factory. There he received a beating from his employer for making a mistake while caning a chair. Learning of his son's beating,
argued with the employer. A scuffle ensued and both
were thrown in jail; an apprentice was given few rights in
decided to run away. When informed of his son's decision,
said, "I don't blame you. I wish I could too."
traveled north about eighty miles to the small seaport of
. He eluded the police, who must have been looking for him, because he broke his contract. Then as a cook's boy he sailed away in
In February 1842, after having risen to the rank of able-bodied seaman,

and back. He writes in his Narrative, "The weather was very cold ... being almost constantly wet ... I suffered very much ... [I] determined to make no more voyages to cold climates." Although being a fugitive from the law caused
to leave home, he now had the incentive to search for a warm climate.

Something of Charley's character is revealed in the next lines of his Narrative. "Here I remained all the summer of 1844 in charge of the store house . . .
was discharged here and again tried to influence
against me by false reports. This time I gave him a good clubbing, very near breaking his arm."
gave up storekeeping and began the very lucrative activity of otter hunting with
was still reporting his occupation as otter hunter on the 1860 Census.
In July 1846 the hunters stopped at
[sic] in possession of the port. There
. After hunting farther north the hunters returned to a camp just above the harbor of
, where they were warned about a band of marauders. Late that night they slipped away from their camp out to sea and safety. "There were several of them headed by an Italian called
, a regular desperado, who was killed two or three days afterwards. ...(members of the band) acknowledged that they intended to kill us all and take our skins."
, they were arrested by Californians (Mexican citizens).
(a German) were released, while the Americans were held.
declined to join either side in the war and continued his otter hunting.
interrupted his otter hunting. He could not get away in 1848, but in March 1849 he went to the

A most significant document appears in the Actions of the
on April 11, 1861. The abridged transcript reads, "I,
which latter name I hereby declare is not my true name ... acknowledge myself to be the father of
aged about eleven years ... of an Indian woman ...
, and I ... hereby adopt ... [them] ... as my own children."
In this document that acknowledged the name Brown to be an alias,

also changed his children's surname Cascareña (not Brown) to Dittmann. Notice that the children's surname had been that of their mother. It seems that he was adopting his own children, perhaps because he was never married to
and she may have died. This document is probably more accurate than the 1860 Census concerning the children's birth order and ages.
may have died during the latter half of 1860 appears in the article, "St. Vincent's Students."6

The Daughters of Charity of St. Vincent de Paul
also accepted "half-orphans," described as having one deceased parent.

While browsing through court records, I discovered a document that greatly complicated Charley Brown's life.
In court proceedings7 that were recorded on August 10, 1852,
was the plaintiff in a child custody case. "... a Writ of Habeas Corpus by

... The Court ordered that the body of the child
